originalTitle: Former Nigerian minister sentenced to 75 years in rare corruption verdict
title: A Story About Rules and Being Fair in Nigeria!
content:
Hello, my wonderful friends all across Africa! It's your friendly news reporter here, ready to share an important story from a very special country called Nigeria. Nigeria is a big, beautiful country in West Africa, full of amazing people, vibrant colours, and exciting sounds! Just like in your homes and schools, Nigeria has rules to help everyone live happily and fairly. Today, we're talking about a big grown-up who broke those rules, and what happened next.

Meet the Grown-Up Helpers!

Imagine your mom or dad, or your teacher, being in charge of something really important for many, many people. That's a bit like what a 'minister' is! A minister is a special grown-up who helps run a part of the country. They have a big job to make sure everything works well for everyone. Mr. Saleh Mamman was a very important minister. He was the 'Power Minister'. Can you guess what he was in charge of? That's right! Electricity! He had the job of making sure homes, schools, and hospitals in Nigeria had electricity. Electricity helps us turn on lights, watch TV, charge our phones, and keep our food cold in the fridge. It’s a super important thing for modern life! Without electricity, many things would be much harder. It's a huge responsibility to make sure everyone has power. Imagine trying to learn your lessons in school without any lights! Or not being able to play your favourite games on a tablet because it can't charge. So, a Power Minister has to be very, very careful and honest with their work. *Did you know?* The first electric power station in Africa was built in Kimberley, South Africa, way back in 1882! It helped power diamond mines and light up a whole town!

What Does "Breaking the Rules" Mean?

Sometimes, grown-ups forget their big responsibilities and decide to be unfair. This is what we call 'corruption'. It means using your special job and trust to take things that don't belong to you, or to make yourself rich, instead of helping everyone else. Think of it like this: If your teacher gave you money to buy snacks for the whole class, and you bought a huge treat for yourself, but only tiny ones for everyone else, that would be unfair, right? You would be using your special job (buying snacks for everyone) to benefit only yourself. That's a bit like corruption. When important grown-ups, like ministers, do this, they are taking resources – like money that should build new schools or fix roads – and keeping it for themselves. This means fewer good things for the children and families in the country. Corruption is like a sneaky little monster that tries to steal good things meant for everyone. It makes things harder for regular people and stops countries from growing strong and healthy. *Did you know?* The word 'corruption' comes from a very old word that means 'to break' or 'to spoil'. When someone is corrupt, they spoil the trust people have in them and break important rules!

A Big Story About Fairness

Our story today is about Mr. Saleh Mamman. He was once the Power Minister in Nigeria. But sadly, the grown-ups who look after fairness and rules, called the 'justice system', found out that he was not playing fair. They found that he had used his important job in a wrong way. The justice system found that Mr. Mamman was involved in corruption. This means he did not use the money and power from his job to help people get electricity or build important things. Instead, he did things that only helped himself or people close to him, which is very wrong. Because of this, the justice system had a big meeting, like a big court where important decisions are made. They looked at all the information carefully. And they decided that Mr. Mamman had broken the rules very seriously. He was 'sentenced' to 75 years! Now, 75 years is a very, very long time! It means that the grown-ups in charge of fairness want to show everyone how serious it is when someone in an important job breaks the rules. It's like being put in 'time-out' for a really, really long time because you did something very wrong. This long sentence is a big message to everyone: no matter how powerful or important you are, you must follow the rules. It shows that the rules are there for everyone, to keep everything fair and just. Right now, the people in charge don't know exactly where Mr. Mamman is. But even if he is not here, the rules are still there. The grown-ups who work for justice will keep looking and working hard to make sure that everyone, no matter how important, faces the consequences of their actions. It shows that the rules apply to everyone, and justice will find a way. *Did you know?* Nigeria is the most populous country in Africa! That means it has more people living there than any other country on our continent! Imagine how many people need electricity there!

Why This Story Matters to All of Us!

You might be thinking, 'Why is this important for me to know?' Well, it's very important! This story shows us that even big, important grown-ups must follow the rules. It shows that there are people working hard to make sure everyone is fair. When someone takes money that should be used for good things, it hurts many, many people. Imagine if that money could have built a new school in your village, or bought more medicine for a hospital, or even helped bring clean water to more homes. But because of corruption, those good things might not happen. So, when someone like Mr. Mamman is found to be corrupt, and then gets sentenced, it's a good sign! It means that the country is trying its best to fix things. It means they are saying, 'No, we will not let this happen. We want our country to be fair and strong!' This helps make Nigeria, and all of Africa, a better place for everyone, especially for children like you. It means that the money and resources of the country can be used for things that truly help people, instead of going into someone's pocket unfairly. When people are honest, countries grow stronger. More schools can be built, more children can get good education, and more families can live healthier and happier lives. This is why fighting corruption is so important! *Did you know?* Justice scales have two pans, one for facts and one for evidence, showing that decisions should be balanced and fair, just like how grown-ups decide if someone broke a rule!

What Can YOU Do?

You might be thinking, 'I'm just a kid, what can I do?' Well, you can do so much! You can start by always being honest and fair in your own life. Always share your toys, don't take things that aren't yours, and always tell the truth. These simple things might seem small, but they are super important! They help you grow into a strong, honest grown-up who will make good choices for your community and your country. Help your friends, follow the rules at school, and be kind to everyone. These are the building blocks of a fair and just world. Every small act of honesty and fairness helps. When you grow up, you might become a teacher, a doctor, an engineer, or even a leader in your community! Remember this story and choose to always do what is right and fair. Be a helper, not someone who takes unfairly. *Did you know?* Being honest means always telling the truth, even when it's hard, and it makes people trust you a lot! Trust is like a special glue that holds friendships and communities together.

Let's Make Africa Strong and Fair!

So, my dear friends, this story reminds us that rules are there to help us all. And when rules are broken, there are consequences, even for very important grown-ups. It shows that Nigeria is working hard to be fair and just for all its people. Let's all promise to do our part, big and small, to make our homes, our schools, and our wonderful continent of Africa even stronger, fairer, and brighter every single day! Until next time, keep shining bright!
summary: A former important helper in Nigeria, Mr. Saleh Mamman, was found to have broken rules by being unfair with money (corruption) and was given a very long sentence, showing that everyone, even big grown-ups, must follow the rules.
whyMatters: This is important because it shows that breaking rules has consequences, and it helps make sure that money and resources are used to help all people in Nigeria, especially for things like schools and hospitals, instead of being taken unfairly by individuals.

originalTitle: 'They shot my neighbour in the head' - the lakeside city traumatised by war
title: Helping Hands and Hope in Uvira: A Story for All Our Friends
content:
Hello, Friends!

Hello, amazing kids across Africa and beyond! It's your friend, the news reporter, here to share a story from a very special place. Today, we are going to talk about a city called Uvira. It's a real city, just like your town or village, but with its own unique stories. Even when grown-ups have big problems, there are always helpers and kind hearts trying to make things better.

Where is Uvira?

Uvira is located in a big, big country. This country is called the Democratic Republic of Congo, or DR Congo for short. Can you say DR Congo? It’s a fun name! DR Congo is one of the largest countries in Africa. Imagine how many schools, playgrounds, and amazing animals like gorillas and okapis are in such a big place! This country is full of wonder.

A City by a Magical Lake

Uvira is very special because it sits right next to an enormous lake. This lake is called Lake Tanganyika. Have you ever seen a lake so big it looks like the ocean? That’s Lake Tanganyika! It is incredibly beautiful, with sparkling blue water and lots of amazing fish swimming inside. People love to fish there and enjoy the calm water. It’s a very important part of the city. Did you know? Lake Tanganyika is the second deepest lake in the whole wide world! It's so deep, you could stack many tall buildings on top of each other and still not reach the bottom! And it has special fish that live nowhere else. It's truly a natural wonder and a beautiful home for many people.

What Happened in Uvira?

Sometimes, even in the most beautiful places, grown-ups can have big disagreements. These disagreements can get very serious, and sometimes they lead to fighting. In December, not too long ago, some different groups of people were fighting near Uvira. When grown-ups fight like this, it can make a lot of noise and make people feel very worried. This big fighting is sometimes called a "conflict" or a "dispute." It means people are not agreeing and are using force instead of talking.

Feeling Scared and Moving Away

When there is fighting, people feel scared. Imagine hearing loud noises and not knowing what is happening. That's how many people in Uvira felt. They wanted to be safe. So, some families had to leave their homes very quickly. They left behind their beds, their toys, and their familiar streets. They went to other places where it was quieter and safer. This is called being "displaced." It means you have to move away from your home because it's not safe anymore. It's a very difficult thing to do, especially for children who miss their schools and their friends. No one wants to leave their home, but sometimes it's important to do to stay safe. It makes people feel sad and unsure about what will happen next.

Why Do People Fight?

It's a tricky question, even for grown-ups. Sometimes, different groups want different things, or they don't agree on how to share things, like land or power. Like when you and your friend both want to play with the same toy, but much, much bigger. These disagreements become very big and serious. It is always better to talk and share, instead of fighting. Everyone wants to live in peace and be treated fairly.

Helping Hands are Everywhere!

But guess what? Even when things are tough, there are always kind people who want to help! Many good people, from Uvira and from other places, rushed to help the families who were scared or had to leave their homes. These helpers are like real-life superheroes, showing bravery and kindness! Some brought yummy food for hungry tummies, so no one had to go to bed hungry. Some gave out warm blankets to keep people cosy, especially the little ones. Others worked hard to make sure children had safe places to play and learn, even if they couldn't go to their regular schools. They made temporary schools and play areas, so kids could still laugh and learn. These wonderful helpers show us that even in hard times, kindness and care shine brightly.

Working for Peace

Many people are working very, very hard to make sure Uvira can be peaceful again. Peace means everyone can live safely, without fear, and be happy together. It means children can go to school, play with their friends, and sleep soundly at night, without any scary noises. It means families can stay together and build a better future. Big groups, like the United Nations, also try to help. Did you know? The United Nations is like a super big club made up of almost all the countries in the world! They work together to try and stop fighting, help people, and make the world a better, more peaceful place for everyone. They send helpers called 'peacekeepers' to talk to the fighting groups and try to find solutions. They believe that talking is always better than fighting.

Hopes for Uvira

Everyone in Uvira wants to go back to their homes. They want to rebuild their lives and their city. They want their beautiful city by Lake Tanganyika to be filled with laughter and joy, not worry. They want to walk to the market without fear and send their children to school every day. They want peace to be strong and lasting. It takes time, like planting a tiny seed and waiting for it to grow into a tall, strong tree. But with lots of care, kindness, and hard work from many people, peace can grow strong in Uvira. We send all our love and hope to the children and families there. We hope they can soon play, learn, and live without any worries, enjoying their amazing city and the beautiful lake. They deserve all the peace and happiness in the world!
summary: In the city of Uvira, DR Congo, near a big lake, some fighting made people scared and sad, but many kind helpers are bringing food, comfort, and working hard to bring peace back.
whyMatters: It's important to know that even when bad things like fighting happen, people always help each other, and everyone deserves to live in a safe and peaceful home, free from fear.

title: Nigerian film star Alexx Ekubo dies from cancer aged 40
content: The actor was recognised for his contributions to entertainment as well as his humanitarian efforts.

originalTitle: BRICS meeting overshadowed by war on Iran
title: Friends from Far Away Meet Up!
content:
Hello, My Little Friends!

Hi everyone! It's Aisha, your friendly news reporter from Africa! I'm here to tell you about some important news. We are going to talk about a special group of friends. These friends come from very far away lands. They want to make our world a better place. Let's learn about them!

What is BRICS? It's a Special Club!

Imagine you have a club with your best friends. You all meet up to play games and share snacks. Well, BRICS is a bit like that! But instead of kids, it's a club for *countries*. Yes, big countries! They are like very big friends who help each other. BRICS stands for five countries that are friends. Let me tell you their names! * **B** stands for **Brazil**. That's a big country very far away, across the ocean. * **R** stands for **Russia**. This is a huge country, the biggest one in the whole wide world! * **I** stands for **India**. It's a very colourful country with many, many people. * **C** stands for **China**. China is also a very big country. It is famous for pandas and big walls! * **S** stands for **South Africa**. And guess what? South Africa is right here in our amazing continent, Africa! Hooray for South Africa!

Why Do These Countries Meet?

These big countries meet to talk about important things. They want to help each other grow. They want to make sure their people have good jobs and nice homes. They talk about sharing ideas. They also talk about how to trade things. Trading means buying and selling things, like when you trade your toys with a friend. When countries trade, they become stronger together. It's like sharing your best ideas to make a cool new game! They want to make life better for everyone in their countries. **Did you know?** South Africa joined the BRICS club in the year 2010! That was a very special year for our country. Being part of BRICS means South Africa can talk with very big countries. They talk about how to make our continent even better.

A Big Meeting with Big Talks

Recently, the leaders from these BRICS countries had a big meeting. Imagine all the leaders sitting together. They had very important discussions. They talked about how to make sure everyone in their countries can live happily and safely. They want to help everyone have good schools and hospitals. **Did you know?** When leaders meet, they often shake hands and smile. It shows they are friends and want to work together. It's a way of saying, "Hello, let's be friends and work together!"

A Worry from Far Away

But guess what? Sometimes, even when friends meet, there can be worries. At this BRICS meeting, the leaders also talked about a big worry. It was about a country very far away, called **Iran**. Sometimes, countries have big problems. It's like when you and your friend don't agree on which game to play. But for countries, these disagreements can be very big. They can make many people feel worried and sad. The BRICS leaders heard news about these worries from Iran. People were talking about how some countries were not agreeing with Iran. This made many people around the world feel a little bit worried. The BRICS leaders knew this was a serious talk. They knew they needed to talk about peace.

What Does "Overshadowed" Mean?

The news said this worry "overshadowed" the meeting. What does that mean? It means that this big worry about Iran was so important. It took a lot of attention. It was like a big, dark cloud trying to cover up the bright sunshine of their good talks. Even though the BRICS leaders wanted to talk about happy things like helping each other, they also had to spend a lot of time talking about this worry. They wanted to find ways to help keep peace for everyone. **Did you know?** Peace means no fighting. It means everyone is safe and happy. It means smiling and sharing with each other.

Why Does This Matter to Us in Africa?

You might think, "Iran is so far away! Why should I care?" That's a good question! Even if a country is far away, what happens there can sometimes affect us a little bit. If there are big problems far away, it can make things like food or fuel for cars cost more everywhere. It can also make people feel sad or scared. Our friends in South Africa, who are part of BRICS, want peace for everyone, everywhere. They want all countries to be friends and talk about their problems, not fight. We are all connected on this big planet Earth. **Did you know?** Africa is the second-largest continent in the world! It has 54 beautiful countries, and South Africa is one of them. We are all part of one big family on Earth.

BRICS Friends Want Peace!

So, what did the BRICS leaders say about this big worry? They said they want **peace**. They want all countries to talk to each other kindly. They believe that talking is always, always better than fighting. They want everyone to be safe and to live without any fear. They talked about how important it is for countries to respect each other. Respect means being kind and understanding. Our South African leaders, being part of this special club, also shared their wish for peace. They want to help make sure that disagreements are solved in a gentle and fair way. **Did you know?** The symbol for peace is often a dove. A dove is a beautiful white bird that flies gently in the sky.

What Can We Do?

Even though we are just kids, we can do our part for peace too! * We can be kind to our friends at school. Share your toys and smiles! * We can share our yummy snacks. * We can help our family members with chores. * When we have disagreements, we can talk about them instead of shouting. Say "I feel sad when..." instead. * Every little act of kindness helps make our world a more peaceful and happy place. The BRICS countries are trying to show big countries how to be good friends. Let's all try to be good friends too! Let's spread kindness everywhere we go.

Conclusion:

The BRICS leaders met as friends, even when there were big worries from far away. They talked about peace and helping each other. They want a safe and happy world for all children, including you! And South Africa is right there, helping them make it happen! Hooray for peace and friendship!
summary: Leaders from special club countries, like South Africa, met to talk about helping each other and peace, even when there were worries about a country far away.
whyMatters: It matters because these countries want everyone to be safe and kind, and problems far away can still touch our lives a little bit. They want a peaceful world for all kids.

originalTitle: Flattery and fanfare as Trump welcomed to China - but thorny issues remain
title: Big Smiles and Important Talks: Leaders Meet in China!
content:
Hello amazing kids! It's your friendly reporter, Zola, here from across Africa! Today, we have a super exciting story about two very big countries and their leaders. Imagine two important grown-ups from very far away lands meeting up for a big chat! That's exactly what happened when the leader of America went to visit the leader of China.

Who is Mr. Trump?

Mr. Trump was the President of a country called America. America is a very big and powerful country, far away from Africa, across the ocean. The President is like the main helper and leader for everyone in his country. He makes big decisions and helps his people. Did you know? The President of America lives in a special house called the White House! It has many, many rooms!

Who is Mr. Xi?

Mr. Xi is the President of China. China is another very, very big country, even bigger than America in some ways! It is far away from Africa too, on another side of the world. Mr. Xi is the main helper and leader for all the people in China. He works hard for his country. Did you know? China is famous for its giant pandas! They are black and white and super cute, and they love to munch on bamboo!

A Big Trip Across the World

So, Mr. Trump, the leader from America, travelled a very, very long way. He went all the way to China! This trip was like visiting a friend's house, but on a much bigger scale. When important leaders travel, it's called a state visit. It's a special time for countries to talk. Did you know? Travelling from America to China can take over 10 hours in an aeroplane! That's a very long flight!

A Super Warm Welcome!

When Mr. Trump arrived in China, he got a truly amazing welcome! Imagine a big party just for him. There were red carpets, just like movie stars walk on. There were lots of flags waving everywhere, showing both America's flag and China's flag. People were cheering, and sometimes there were special parades. This big, happy welcome is called 'fanfare' and 'flattery'. It means everyone was trying to be extra nice and friendly to show respect. It's like when you have a very special guest at home and you make sure everything is perfect for them. Did you know? Sometimes, leaders exchange special gifts to show friendship. Imagine getting a unique gift from another country!

Smiling for the Cameras

When Mr. Trump and Mr. Xi met, they smiled a lot. They smiled for all the cameras! The cameras were there to take pictures and videos so everyone in the world could see them. Even if grown-ups have different ideas sometimes, they always try to look friendly and polite in front of everyone. They shook hands firmly and looked happy to see each other. It's important for leaders to show the world that they can get along, even when things are a bit tricky.

What are 'Thorny Issues'?

Now, here's the tricky part! The news said there were 'thorny issues'. What does 'thorny' mean? Imagine a rose with sharp thorns. They can prick you! So, 'thorny issues' means there were some problems or disagreements that were a bit difficult, like thorns. It's like when you and your friend might not agree on everything, but you still want to be friends. Countries also have things they don't always agree on. These big leaders wanted to talk about these tough things.

Talking About Buying and Selling Things (Trade)

One of the big 'thorny issues' was about 'trade'. What is trade? It's when countries buy and sell things to each other. For example, China might make many toys, and America might buy them. America might make special aeroplanes, and China might buy them. Sometimes, one country might feel that the other country is selling too many things to them, or not buying enough from them. It's like if you share your toys, and one friend feels they are not getting a fair share. So, Mr. Trump and Mr. Xi talked about making sure this buying and selling was fair for both countries. Did you know? Many of the clothes and toys we use every day come from countries like China!

Talking About a Far Away Neighbour

Another tricky issue was about a country called North Korea. It's a country very close to China. North Korea was doing things that made many countries, including America, worried. They were making big rockets and doing things that made people feel unsafe. Mr. Trump wanted Mr. Xi's help to talk to North Korea and ask them to stop doing these worrying things. It's like when your neighbour is doing something that makes everyone in the street a bit scared, and you ask a grown-up to help talk to them. Did you know? North Korea is separated from its neighbour, South Korea, by a special border where soldiers watch very carefully.

Trying to Work Together

Even with these 'thorny issues', both leaders knew it was very important to talk. They wanted to find ways to work together. When two very big countries like America and China get along, it makes the whole world a safer and happier place. They promised to keep talking and try to solve their problems. It's like when you and your friend have a disagreement, but you talk about it nicely to stay friends. That's what grown-up leaders try to do too!

Why This Meeting Matters to Us

Why is this important for us, all the way here in Africa? Because America and China are two of the biggest countries in the world. What they do affects everyone! If they are friendly and work together, it helps the whole world stay peaceful and healthy. It means more people can get along, and that's good for every country, including ours! Did you know? There are almost 200 countries in the world! Each one has a leader who works to make their country better. So, remember, kids: even important grown-ups have 'thorny issues', but talking and trying to be friends is always the best way! This is Zola, your Africa news reporter, signing off! See you next time!
